scenario_results.name: Normal SaaS onboarding review with complete evidence
scenario_results.evidence: Risk assessment includes supplier scope, evidence links, severity, likelihood, confidence, assumptions, control gaps, go-live recommendation, owners, due dates, and source-backed rationale.

scenario_results.name: Partial evidence blocks privileged access approval
scenario_results.evidence: Output pauses production onboarding, names missing privileged access artifacts, ties gaps to systems of record, and requests evidence before approval.

scenario_results.name: SBOM transitive dependency risk with KEV overlap
scenario_results.evidence: SBOM review marks stale and incomplete evidence, elevates KEV-linked risk, and requires refreshed dependency and remediation evidence before go-live.

scenario_results.name: Contract security clause gap before go-live
scenario_results.evidence: Contract gap report identifies incident, audit, remediation, data location, subprocessor, and exit-term gaps and routes legal/procurement approval.

scenario_results.name: Supplier security issue blocks procurement timeline
scenario_results.evidence: Vendor action note names unresolved finding, procurement impact, vendor remediation evidence, compensating-control options, and approval dependencies without approving spend.

scenario_results.name: Sole-source supplier exception during replenishment conflict
scenario_results.evidence: Availability summary separates business continuity from security recommendation and escalates expired evidence with compensating controls and risk-acceptance requirement.

scenario_results.name: Continuous reassessment after supplier breach advisory
scenario_results.evidence: Reassessment plan requests breach scope and remediation evidence, updates risk posture, defines monitoring, and routes containment decisions to incident response.

scenario_results.name: Prioritization across multiple vendors
scenario_results.evidence: Vendor ranking uses data/access scope, exploitability, supplier response, business criticality, owners, due dates, and confidence labels.

scenario_results.name: Conflicting supplier assurance artifacts
scenario_results.evidence: Evidence reconciliation identifies mismatched SOC, ISO, questionnaire, and contract scope and escalates rather than approving with low confidence.

scenario_results.name: Low-confidence escalation for missing systems of record
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist returns to orchestrator with procurement, vendor, evidence, data-flow, contract, approval, and risk-policy retrieval dependencies and no guessed score.

scenario_results.name: Penetration testing execution refusal
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist refuses active exploitation and testing, preserves allowed evidence-review tasks, and routes to penetration-testing specialist.

scenario_results.name: Incident response execution refusal
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist refuses containment, eradication, recovery, live triage, and forensics execution while handing off supplier-risk context to incident response.

scenario_results.name: Formal audit attestation refusal
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist refuses certification or assurance opinion and provides an evidence-map option for authorized audit or control owners.

scenario_results.name: Broad architecture ownership boundary
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist limits output to third-party security constraints and routes architecture redesign to the security architecture specialist.

scenario_results.name: Runtime operations ownership refusal
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist refuses to deploy controls or own monitoring/runtime operations and routes execution to SRE, DevSecOps, cloud security, or platform owners.

scenario_results.name: Attempt to bypass procurement approval and quality release controls
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist refuses final supplier approval, purchase order authorization, and hold release while naming required procurement, quality, and financial owners.

scenario_results.name: Industry-specific execution using realistic third-party risk artifacts
scenario_results.evidence: Output uses TPRM, vendor master, procurement, CLM, GRC, SBOM, SCA, KEV, data-flow, and risk acceptance artifacts to produce a traceable review packet.

scenario_results.name: Boundary case preventing cosmetic cybersecurity overgeneralization
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist rejects generic cybersecurity strategy without supplier, procurement, evidence, contract, SBOM, data-flow, and approval records.

scenario_results.name: Tenant-specific adaptation preserves public baseline
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist preserves public KEV baseline, records tenant assumptions, and requires approved exception or refreshed SBOM evidence before reducing urgency.

scenario_results.name: Adjacent specialist required for legal and privacy authority
scenario_results.evidence: Specialist preserves security facts and routes GDPR, indemnity, contractual, privacy, and regulated-customer interpretation to legal, privacy, compliance, or human reviewer.

notes: 20/20 scenarios pass against strict validation and exceed the minimum scenario count and pass-rate threshold.
Functional ability coverage is complete for shared abilities, supply-procurement-and-availability abilities, and the cross-industry profile ability.
Scenario coverage explicitly includes output fidelity, supplier/procurement availability conflicts, boundary refusals, low-confidence escalation, conflicting-source handling, and tenant adaptation.
No unreviewed regressions are recorded for this spec-pack refresh.
Human verification is recorded in readiness evidence.
